speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four with Walter Austerer. You had just arrived-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bradford, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bradford. They had picked you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yorkshire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Was it Yorkshire? And how many-- were you all alone at that point or were you still with other children from the-- from the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- yes, I believe there were a couple of other children who did go to Bradford. There was a hostel in Bradford. The Jewish rabbi there, a very nice man, had been very active already. And they established a hostel there. And there were children who stayed at that hostel. Eventually, I used to go there. I used to play ping pong there. And I met some other people. And there was one German couple who-- whose son-- they had come over as domestics, I believe. And they opened their house every Sunday or Saturday maybe, on Sabbath, I don't remember, to kids. And I used to go there frequently for that one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was just thinking about it. I don't remember it any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and so what was life like now in your new residence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- you know, it was strange. I was-- there were-- the young couple was very nice. But they were newlyweds and I felt I was a real intruder there. But they were very gracious, very, very nice. The British were wonderful. Everyone-- and everyone I met was really remarkable, very kind, and very, very, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stayed there. And then they started a school. Did I go to school in England or in Lancashire? No. I didn't-- I didn't go to school in Bradford.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened-- yeah. I didn't go to school in Bradford. I just-- I don't recall, because I was apprenticed to a-- Mr. Hurst, had a-- he was a councilman, among other things. He had two huge plants, garment manufacturing plants. He was also a councilman in City of Bradford and had a beautiful home. And he had a friend who was a contractor, an electrical contractor. And he apprenticed me out to this electrical contractor. And I don't recall now whether that was after I was evacuated or whether I started before. But I don't think I went to school in Bradford, so I didn't do very much there, from what I recall. But it was a short time, because by September-- I was there two months. And by September 4, I think, or 6, the war started. And I remember listening to King George proclaiming, announcing the beginning of war with Germany. And then very soon afterwards, we were evacuated into a-- into the countryside in a very small town called Colne, Lancashire, C-O-- C-O-L-N-E, Lancashire. And as I mentioned to you privately before, I can't remember-- again, I-- I repressed it and I can't remember how I got there. I just can't remember whether by train, by bus, by cart, whatever. And-- and we were-- but I do remember we wound up in a schoolyard. And the parents came. And they knew we were-- children were coming. Those were British children too, many more British than-- than refugees. And they were coming. And the-- the parents came, people and couples came there and picked out the children they wanted to take to their homes. This was what-- how it was done. These children who were evacuated wound up in other people's homes. And so people came and selected the-- the children. Well, two or three of us were very tall, much taller for our age than different age group, I remember. So we were left over and nobody took us. And then finally, the pub owner took two of us, the pub in-- in Colne, Lancashire. And so we went to the pub. And they showed us the room. The room had a rubber sheet on it, because they expected little kids who were still bedwetting. And I remember that vividly, because we were laughing about it. And they were also very, very nice. But we didn't want to be there, because, you know, the pub and the dart throwing and the noise. So [LAUGHS] it wasn't happy. So a couple of the refugee kids, one-- one of the refugee kids, came back two day-- a day later or two days later, to the pub. And I had gotten to know him then. And he said, you know, I walked up that country lane, and I knocked on the door of that-- that house at the end of the lane and asked the people to take me in, and they did. And so I think you should do that too. So I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I can't believe that I did it. When I think back, I say, how did I-- you know, I was 15 years old. I barely spoke the language. How on earth did I do it? So I don't remember by myself or I walked with another guy. I walked up that beautiful British country land. At the end of it, there was a golf course and the moors, the-- the moors started, a beautiful spot. And I knocked on the-- I don't know whether I knocked on one door or two doors, but I knocked on the door, and also very close to that other-- a couple by the name of Hindle, H-I-N-D-L-E, had one boy about two years younger than me, or three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me in. They took me in. And I stayed with them for, oh, maybe five months or so. And they took me everywhere. He was a banker and worked for bank vice president. And she came from a very wealthy family. And they took me everywhere they went to, to her parents and walks and what have you. They were very-- and there I started school. And a typically British schoolmaster, who that once took us to his mother's thatched house for tea, all the-- all the students. And there I began to really pick up the-- the language and became much more fluent.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that you were living with, were they Jewish or non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish. While this time--

speaker: interviewee
text: So was Mr. Hurst.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up until now and the time that you've been in England through living with these people, was there any news from-- from Vienna? Were you getting any communication?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to get-- well, my mother was in the United States already, so I got letters from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her papers came through and she--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was beginning to get letters from her, yeah. Well, I got letters anyway, because till the war, she was still able to write to me from Vienna. Then she left about October, I think, of that year. So once she came to United States, she-- even though the war had started, she was still able to get letters. And then I got one or two letters after my father-- what my father did, he left on an illegal transport also, probably October, maybe, after my mother left. So he waited for my mother to leave and then he got on the next illegal transport to Palestine. And then the Danube froze over that winter, in '39, and they were taken off the ship. That was in Yugoslavia. They were taken off the ship and put into a camp in [INAUDIBLE], Yugoslavia, which is about between-- I went there in '69. It's between Belgrade and the coast, about almost halfway, closer to Belgrade. And they were put into a camp by the Yugoslavs. And they stayed in that camp. And while he was in that camp, he-- through the Red Cross he would write to me, very-- not often. But I have a couple of his letters. And one letter, he asked me the possibility to-- if he had 2,000 pounds, he would be able-- they would let him go to England, I think, wherever. Well, I had no way-- I couldn't even appeal to people to put down 10,000-- 2,000 pounds, $10,000. And so then the Nazis came into Yugoslavia in '41. And they exterminated the entire-- there were 800 people in the camp, taken off that ship. And they were all exterminated. And they are now buried in a mass grave in Belgrade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how that was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the information I got from the Yugoslav Committee, the letter came from the Yugoslav Jewish community after the war, telling us about it, was that they were just shot. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you're-- you're in England, going to school with-- with-- with English children. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe you-- you said you were a refugee. How was the relationship between the refugee children and the-- between yourself and-- and the other children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I'm trying to think whether-- whether it was just refugee children. I think it was-- it might have been just-- no, it wasn't, because we-- no. I'm not sure about that either. I know we played soccer with-- with British kids. And we could barely understand, because the Yorkshire accent is very, very sharp, difficult to understand. For instance, 'take it,' they'll say 'tak it, tak it, tak it.' Another-- knowing 'take it,' I-- I had to learn 'tak it.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I don't remember. I remember playing soccer with them. Now, whether we were in our own-- I-- I would think that we were in our own class that we did, that there were refugee kids. Yeah. And we had our own class. While you were in England, were you able to observe any Jewish practices?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that? How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm wondering at this point-- and I haven't thought about that, but because-- it must have been very close to High Holidays. But I think we were already evacuated into Colne, Lancashire, and there wouldn't have been any chance of-- because it was too small a town. I don't think there were Jewish-- I wasn't aware of any Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us what happened while you were in-- in school in Colne, how long you stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed there for about four or five months, till-- again, I don't remember how I got back to Bradford-- till the government decided that there would be no danger of imminent-- of bombings at that point. I mean, there were sporadic bombings going on, but no mass destruction. And the children went back to their original homes at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went back to Bradford, and I-- back to the same couple. And then I apprenticed to that-- I was apprenticed to that electrician. And I did a little bit of work, very little, because they had very little-- what happened, the private-- the-- the private work stopped during the war and the government work hadn't started yet, so they were sort of in between, and I didn't-- I worked in the office a little bit, I did this, but very-- very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a couple of dollars, a couple of pounds or whatever, not pounds, but whatever money, I did get a little money, earned a little bit of money, so I had some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Bradford at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that time, I was in Bradford till April, 1940. And I-- my affidavit was coming through at that point, and I had to go to London by myself in the blackout. England was totally blacked out at that point, totally. And so I went on a train and went to London. And the [? Heiers ?] put me up in one of their houses that they had, that they kept for that purpose, and I guess fed me too. And I went to the embassy, United States embassy, and had to apply or do whatever for my visa. And I had to go back to Bradford. And it took, I don't know, another month or so before I had to go back again to pick up my visa. And then I left in April via Southampton and again went to London and went to Southampton. And the ship, it was a Dutch ship, the Rotterdam. And it didn't-- by that time, Holland was not in the war yet, hadn't been invaded, so they were a still neutral nation. There was a neutrality zone, which meant that these ships could not-- and England was at war, so the ships could not get into an English harbor. So they had to stay about three miles outside the harbor. Well, the ship would lay, and they put us up in a-- in a hotel. And they paid for the hotel, but we had to pay for our own food. And I remember there was a British gentleman who was coming to the United States, old-- older man, and he had family, or whatever. But he was going to also leave the country, come to the United States. And he and I sat at the same table. And there was this huge dining room. And I had-- I came to United States with $12. And he had to order dinner. And I was faced with the choice of-- of spending my money or saving something for coming here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I remember feeling very, very uncomfortable. It was the first time I had been in a restaurant on my own. And I still remember that. And so I did order something, not much, because I still had some money left. And then I took the ship to United States, and my mother waited for me at the pier. Prior to leaving Southampton on-- on the ship, you were still communicating, you were still getting letters from your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From my father, I got-- yes. I got letters from my mother, very-- not often. But there was-- there was enough communication so that she knew I was-- what was happening, I was getting my visa and so on. But the ones-- my father, his letters didn't come through anymore at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there any news of what was happening? Did your mother have any news? Did-- so you just didn't-- did you know what was happening to him at the time? To him at the time-- I knew he was at that camp. I knew he was in the camp. That I knew. But there was-- I left in '40, so that was before-- he was still there. That was before he was killed. How long was the ocean voyage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ocean voyage with about seven days, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you have with you on the trip still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the same things I had. I didn't buy any clothes in England. So I had basically, I suppose, the same clothes. Must have-- must have been long enough to-- for me to be able to wear, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And still the same steamer trunk that your father had made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same steamer trunk, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city in-- in America did you come into?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hoboken. The ship landed in Hoboken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what happened when you landed and you met your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it-- it was very strange, you know, a strange feeling, knowing that we were not a family anymore. And my mother had terrible difficulty. She was not able to-- very difficult for her to discuss her feelings with me, maybe with other people. With me, she-- she really never-- because she was very tearful and she would-- afraid of breaking down. And-- and so she just kept it all to herself. She-- she was working a domestic at the time. And my uncle and aunt who had come here, were-- they still-- they had a little-- had a-- we had a little house. OK. They-- they-- she was working as a domestic, and so I didn't see much of her, because she worked. And then on Sundays-- and I-- I-- I lived-- initially, I lived with a-- one of my father's brothers in Bayonne, New Jersey. And another brother was in Perth Amboy. And the third one lived in the Bronx. And there was also a-- a-- another nephew-- no, an aunt, I think, of my father's. But they were also-- but she was also the same age as the brothers for whatever reason. I don't know what the relationship was, but she was there. And so first I lived with my-- with one of his brothers who had four children. And I didn't feel very comfortable there. And-- and then I-- and also, you know, I-- it took me years to get over the anger that I felt for my uncles, his brothers, not to have sent him the affidavit. He really appealed to them, and they-- they would have saved his life. And I felt very, very angry.  It took me a long time. So I-- I put all that anger on them. And-- and then I stayed-- I wanted to come back to New York, and I stayed with this uncle and aunt of-- of my father's, but who were the same age as-- as the other uncles, and-- the real uncles. And they had one daughter. And they were lovely people. They were just wonderful people. And I-- they lived in the Bronx. He was a cutter in the garment industry. He was a rabid Socialist, and very, very good people. And I stayed with them for a little bit. And then I moved in with my uncle and aunt on my mother's side, who had come over from Vienna, from England, as a matter of fact. They also went to England and then came over. And then they rented half a house in Hollis and I-- and had two sons. And I stayed with them. And I stayed with them, then they moved to Forest Hills. And I stayed with them till I got into the army. And then after I got into the army, my mother got an apartment next door to my uncle and aunt, Otto and [? Hedy. ?] And then I, after the war, lived with my mother next door to my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I could go back for a second. These brothers of your father who you felt could've sent an affidavit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a conversation with them trying to understand why or did you ever question them on their actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I didn't. I mean, they-- ostensibly, they were struggling very much in those days. But there was no reason why they even then couldn't have done it. There was no reason. I-- I still don't feel-- I mean, I've forgiven them. They're not alive anymore. But if my brother-- I don't have any brothers, but if my brother would say, I want to-- if I were in that situation, I would send them an affidavit. They were-- they were fearful that he would become another burden. I mean, it wouldn't have happened. My father was very willing to work hard and-- and so on, and he was inventive and I'm sure he would have made it. But I think there was no excuse for-- for not doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up until the time that you joined the army-- was it the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- how did you primarily occupy your time? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I did was as soon as I got here, and I was what, 16, and I had the two uncles, one uncle and his-- his brother-in-law were in the fur business. And so they said, why don't you get a job? We'll get you an apprentice job in the fur business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- and I think I first would have had to get a card, because you're not allowed to work at 16 or whatever, but I did. And they got me a job. But it was highly unionized. And in order to-- to have a job, you had to have a union card. But in order to have a union card, you had to have a job, so it was catch-22. So the union agent came after I worked half a day and either somebody reported me or whatever. And they said, out. So they had connections, and eventually they got me the union card. So I worked there till I got into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to school at night. The first school I went to was just-- I think I learned typing or stenography in those days. And it was some kind of an evening school. And then I started to go high-- finish my high school-- to get my high school diploma. And I went to Jamaica Evening High School while I was working. And I didn't need many credits, because that school was rated so high, that gymnasium was rated so highly, that I needed very little. I needed economics and that kind of stuff and American history and English. And I went for about a year, I think. And maybe I started before '42, and I finished January '43. And two days after I finished, I got my diploma, I went into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when-- during the time from when you came to the United States, and-- what was your reception by the people, the Americans? How did they receive you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, some people were very nice. I remember a school teacher in Bayonne, New Jersey, when I stayed in Bayonne, New Jersey. And he wanted to teach me baseball. And what amazed me was that I would call him by his first name. He's just one of the regular guys. You know, in Europe, this didn't exist. Teacher was on another-- on a different plateau, a different level. You didn't associate with buddy-buddy thing. And so-- so that was one of the introductions to American democracy. I still remember that very vividly. And I don't think he made me a good baseball player, but he tried anyway. And I remember one of extended family, my uncles in Bayonne are some lovely people, very, very, very nice. And they had a daughter, whom I became friendly with, eventually took out. And-- her name was Libby. And so they were very nice. You know, people were very nice. In-- in the business itself, in the fur business, I met-- there were a couple of black people there, black workers. And they did not look upon me very kindly. They did not like my coming in there. And they realized I was getting jobs because of my uncle's influence. And-- One was OK. The other one was not-- two of them were not very nice. So that was kind of difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: In general, did-- did people-- were you able to speak to people of your experiences in Europe when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Sure. To my family. And did I speak to other-- well, strictly it was-- I mean, I was basically just with my family, you know, my extended family. These were the contacts that-- that I made initially-- so I initially didn't meet many other people, except people at work. And it was never-- it wasn't discussed there. It wasn't discussed, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they-- they knew what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did they know what was going on here? Yes. They knew. They knew. They had some idea. That was just the beginning. It was the beginning, before, you know, the camps. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they fully know and wanted to know? No. No. No. I don't think my uncles really dug into that to any great extent. They knew, but it was far away. And-- Tell us about the circumstances of you coming into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was interesting. In '43-- and I'd heard about-- I'd heard about the Austrian battalion. And very little, very little known-- is known about that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we take a break here and we'll start that one the next tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
